summary:
The use of lime plaster to create smooth, durable floor and wall surfaces was one of the early technological achievements of Pre-Pottery Neolithic communities in the Levant and Anatolia, appearing at sites as early as 9000–8500 BCE. Lime plaster floors — made by spreading and smoothing a paste of slaked quicklime (produced by burning limestone) to create a hard, waterproof, polishable surface — have been found at numerous PPNA and PPNB sites including [[event:2|Jericho]], [[event:7|Ain Ghazal]], and [[event:1265|Asikli Hoyuk]].

The production of lime plaster requires burning limestone at temperatures above 850°C — a pyrotechnological achievement that predates pottery firing by several thousand years in the Levantine sequence, demonstrating that high-temperature kiln technology was mastered in the service of architectural and symbolic needs before it was applied to the production of ceramic vessels. Plaster floors were sometimes painted with geometric red or black designs, adding a decorative dimension to their functional role. The investment of skilled labor and fuel required to produce lime plaster floors suggests that these were prestige or special-function surfaces, used in high-status domestic spaces, burial contexts, or communal buildings. The [[event:1324|early lime plaster technology]] represented by these floors became a foundation for later developments in architecture and construction across the ancient Near East.
